class_schedule.topic: Internship Orientation: Professional Development in the Literary Field
learning_objectives: Understand the academic requirements, grading criteria, and deliverables for the internship course
Set professional development goals aligned with career aspirations in publishing, writing, education, or cultural organizations
Establish communication protocols with faculty advisor and site supervisor
readings: Sample internship reflection journals from previous semesters

lecture_notes.introduction: This course connects your literary education to professional experience. Your internship at a publishing house, literary magazine, cultural organization, bookstore, or educational institution is a real professional position with real expectations. This weekly seminar provides structure for reflection, professional development, and connecting your academic training to your career.
key_concepts: Internship requirements: minimum 15 hours/week at an approved literary or cultural organization
Weekly journal expectations: structured reflection connecting daily work to course knowledge
Professional development goals using the SMART framework
Communication protocol: bi-weekly check-ins with faculty advisor
Ethical obligations: confidentiality, professional conduct, and representing the university
lecture_notes.demonstration: Goal-setting workshop: each student drafts three SMART professional development goals for their internship and receives peer and faculty feedback
setup_requirements: Contact information for site supervisor submitted
Digital journal set up and shared with faculty advisor

review.value: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound objectives that transform vague aspirations ('get better at editing') into trackable development targets ('copy-edit 10 manuscript pages per week with fewer than 2 errors per page by week 8')

Internship sites include publishing houses, literary magazines and journals, university presses, bookstores, libraries, arts organizations, nonprofit literacy programs, and content organizations — each connecting literary skills to different professional contexts

class_schedule.topic: Translating Academic Skills to Professional Practice
learning_objectives: Identify how literary analysis, close reading, and writing skills translate to professional contexts
Develop strategies for adapting to organizational culture, tools, and workflows
Document early observations about the gap between academic training and professional practice
readings: Selected essays on careers in the humanities (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your literary education has given you skills that the professional world values but does not always name the same way. Close reading is attention to detail. Literary analysis is critical thinking. Writing about literature is constructing evidence-based arguments. This session helps you translate your academic skills into professional language.
key_concepts: Skills translation: how academic literary skills map onto professional competencies
Organizational adaptation: learning the culture, tools, and expectations of your site
The first-week challenge: being productive while learning everything from scratch
Professional communication: email etiquette, meeting behavior, and status updates
Documenting learning: using your journal to capture insights while they are fresh
lecture_notes.demonstration: Skills mapping exercise: students create a two-column chart mapping academic skills (close reading, thesis construction, source evaluation) to professional applications (editing, proposal writing, content evaluation) at their internship site
setup_requirements: List of tools and processes used at internship site

review.value: 'Close reading' becomes 'attention to detail and nuance.' 'Literary analysis' becomes 'critical thinking and evidence-based reasoning.' 'Writing about literature' becomes 'clear, structured professional communication.' The skills are the same; the professional vocabulary is different.

Every workplace has unwritten rules about communication style, decision-making, dress, and hierarchy. Observing and adapting to these norms quickly — without losing your own identity — is a professional skill as important as any technical competency.

class_schedule.topic: The Editorial Pipeline: From Submission to Publication
learning_objectives: Understand the editorial workflow at your internship site from a participant's perspective
Identify where your skills contribute to the organization's mission
Document specific professional skills you are developing through the internship
readings: Selected industry articles on editorial workflows (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: By now you have been at your internship long enough to see the workflow — the system by which work moves through the organization. Understanding that system is the key to contributing effectively and identifying where your skills are most needed.
key_concepts: Editorial workflows: how content moves from submission through review, editing, production, and distribution
Identifying your role: where you fit in the organizational workflow and how your work connects to the final product
Professional skill development: naming and documenting the skills you are building
Seeking feedback: how to ask for constructive criticism from your supervisor
Building professional relationships: networking authentically at your internship site
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workflow mapping: each student diagrams the editorial or organizational workflow at their site, identifies where they contribute, and presents it to the seminar for comparison across different organizations
setup_requirements: Understanding of site workflow developed through observation

review.value: Diagramming the path content takes from submission to publication reveals the organization's values, priorities, and decision-making structure — and shows you where your work fits in the larger picture
Asking 'What is one thing I could improve this week?' is more productive than 'How am I doing?' — specific, actionable feedback accelerates professional development

learning_objectives: Assess progress toward professional development goals with specific evidence
Identify the most significant professional lessons learned in the first half
Adjust goals and strategies for the remaining weeks

lecture_notes.introduction: The midpoint is the time for honest assessment. What have you learned? What skills have you developed? What goals need adjusting? The most valuable reflection connects specific experiences to broader professional development.
key_concepts: Goal progress review: measuring against SMART objectives with specific evidence
Unexpected learning: skills and insights you did not anticipate
Goal revision: adjusting targets based on what you have learned about yourself and the field
Supervisor feedback integration: incorporating external assessment into self-evaluation
Connecting internship experience to career direction: what this experience tells you about your future
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mid-term presentations: each student gives a 5-minute presentation on their internship experience, key learnings, and adjusted goals, followed by peer and faculty feedback

review.value: Claims about growth must be supported by specific examples: not 'I got better at editing' but 'In week 2, my copyedits had 15 errors per page; by week 7, I averaged 3, based on supervisor review'
Changing your goals halfway through is not failure — it is evidence that you have learned something. The ability to revise plans based on experience is itself a professional skill.

learning_objectives: Translate internship work into portfolio-ready professional samples
Develop a professional narrative connecting academic training, internship experience, and career goals
Update resume and professional profiles to reflect internship competencies

lecture_notes.introduction: Your internship is career capital — but only if you document it effectively. Today we work on translating your experience into professional portfolio pieces and a career narrative that connects your literary education to your professional future.
key_concepts: Portfolio curation: selecting and contextualizing internship work samples
Confidentiality compliance: how to showcase your work without violating agreements
The professional narrative: a coherent story connecting education, internship, and career direction
Resume and LinkedIn updates: incorporating internship experience with specific, quantifiable achievements
Networking after the internship: maintaining professional relationships
lecture_notes.demonstration: Portfolio workshop: students practice writing NDA-compliant descriptions of their internship work, focusing on skills demonstrated and problems solved rather than proprietary details

review.value: Your career narrative connects the dots: literary education gave you close reading, analytical thinking, and communication skills; your internship demonstrated how those skills apply professionally; your next step builds on both
Describe the type of problem you solved, the skills you applied, and the impact of your work without revealing proprietary content, unpublished manuscripts, or confidential business information

learning_objectives: Deliver a professional presentation synthesizing the internship experience
Articulate a clear post-graduation career strategy informed by internship experience
Provide constructive feedback to peers on their presentations

lecture_notes.introduction: Your final presentation synthesizes fifteen weeks of professional experience into a coherent narrative. This is practice for the conversations you will have in interviews when asked about your experience — and a moment to celebrate what you have accomplished.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: context, contributions, challenges, growth, and career impact
Quantifying contributions: edited pages, articles reviewed, events organized, content published
Storytelling in professional contexts: turning daily work into a compelling narrative
Career planning: what you will pursue, what you will avoid, and what you still need to learn
Professional relationship maintenance: staying connected with colleagues and supervisors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Formal presentations: each student delivers a 10-minute final presentation to faculty and peers, followed by 5 minutes of Q&A
setup_requirements: Thank-you notes drafted for site supervisor

review.value: Distill 15 weeks into 10 minutes: what was your role, what did you accomplish, what challenged you, what did you learn, and how does this experience shape your professional direction?
A brief thank-you note and occasional check-ins maintain the professional relationships you built during your internship for future references, collaborations, and opportunities

major_assignments.title: Internship Plan and Goals
major_assignments.description: Develop a structured internship plan defining your professional development goals, communication protocols, and learning objectives. Include a skills gap analysis mapping your academic preparation against your internship role requirements.
objectives: Define three to five SMART professional development goals
Establish communication protocols with supervisor and faculty advisor
Research the organization's mission, culture, and workflow
Create a learning plan for skills required by the role
deliverables: Internship plan document (4-5 pages) with SMART goals, communication plan, and learning objectives
Skills gap analysis mapping academic skills to role requirements
Signed internship agreement and supervisor contact information
Initial journal entry documenting expectations and first impressions

criteria: Goals are specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound
Goals reflect genuine professional development aspirations
Goals are informed by research into the organization and role
At least one goal addresses a known skill gap
Skills gap analysis is honest and specific
Learning plan includes concrete resources and timelines
Document is well-written and professionally formatted
All required forms and agreements are completed
Well-defined SMART goals demonstrating genuine professional development intent
Thorough preparation with organizational research and skills gap analysis
Clear communication plan with all stakeholders

major_assignments.title: Mid-Term Reflection
major_assignments.description: Write a comprehensive mid-term reflection assessing progress toward professional development goals, documenting key learnings, and adjusting plans for the second half of the internship.
objectives: Assess progress toward each SMART goal with specific evidence
Document the most significant professional lessons learned
Address unexpected challenges and how they were handled
deliverables: Updated SMART goals with revisions highlighted and justified

criteria: Honest assessment with specific evidence for each goal
Changes are justified with specific reasoning
New goals remain SMART and achievable
Reflection connects daily tasks to broader professional development
Lessons are generalizable beyond the specific internship
Clear, concise, and within time limit
Professional insight connecting experience to career development
Effective communication through writing and presentation

major_assignments.title: Final Report and Presentation
major_assignments.description: Produce a comprehensive final report synthesizing the entire internship experience, including professional growth documentation, portfolio additions, and career impact assessment. Deliver a formal presentation summarizing the internship journey.
objectives: Synthesize the full internship experience into a professional narrative
Document specific contributions and portfolio-worthy work
Assess the internship's impact on career direction
deliverables: Final report (8-12 pages) covering contributions, growth, challenges, and career impact
Portfolio additions: at least two work samples or case studies from the internship
Thank-you correspondence for supervisor and colleagues

criteria: Comprehensive narrative covering the full internship arc
Challenges discussed honestly with lessons learned
Career impact assessment grounded in experience
At least two substantive work samples included
Well-structured and engaging within time limit
Key contributions and growth communicated effectively
Comprehensive final report synthesizing a rich internship experience
Portfolio-ready work samples demonstrating professional contributions

internship_performance.description: Supervisor evaluation of work quality, professionalism, and growth

weekly_journals.description: Weekly reflection journals documenting learning and connecting to literary education

final_report.description: Comprehensive internship report with portfolio additions

presentation.description: Final presentation to faculty summarizing internship experience

seminar_participation.description: Quality of seminar participation and peer feedback

course_policies.attendance: Attendance at the weekly Friday seminar is mandatory. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a grade reduction. Internship site attendance follows the schedule agreed upon with your supervisor.
course_policies.late_submissions: Weekly journals due every Friday by 5 PM; late journals penalized 10% per day. Major deadlines are firm; extensions require advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: All course deliverables are individual work reflecting your personal internship experience. Peer feedback in seminars is encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be your own. Work documented in reports and portfolio must comply with any confidentiality agreements. Consult your supervisor before including proprietary material in any deliverable.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Digital journal platform, word processing software, presentation tools, and LMS access.
